trajectory_summary: The call opened warm and cooperative — Doug was forthcoming, confirmed the inquiry, and volunteered meaningful context. Carter gathered basic qualifiers efficiently but never converted the energy Doug offered into appointment momentum. The call ends with a soft callback promise that depends entirely on Doug's memory and follow-through — a mechanism Doug himself twice flagged as unreliable. The outcome is structurally fragile.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the practical outcome should be classified as a conditional callback, not a booked appointment. No time was locked, no direct contact with the decision-maker was established, and the relay mechanism (Doug briefing his wife) is compromised by Doug's disclosed cognitive decline. This lead is at high risk of going cold before the next touch.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Callback plan discipline when the relay mechanism is compromised
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So it's all timers thing coming in.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Doug disclosed cognitive decline twice and Carter built a callback plan that depends entirely on Doug's memory. This is not an empathy coaching point alone — it is a structural failure that puts the lead at high risk of going cold. The manager should walk Carter through what a locked callback looks like when the primary contact cannot reliably relay a message: get the wife's direct contact early, offer to reach out directly, and use the email already collected to send a confirmation that bypasses the relay entirely. This is the single most actionable fix on this call.

key_moments.quote: I'm 74. I'm retired, and I'm on a limited income.
key_moments.stewart_read: Doug volunteers age, retirement status, and financial constraint in the first 30 seconds — unprompted. This is simultaneously a bill-anchor opportunity and a vulnerability disclosure. Carter's response ('Are your bills pretty high, your electric bills?') skips the human moment entirely and jumps to data collection. The schema is explicit: vulnerability disclosure is a trust gift, not an interruption to procedure. The correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value. Carter did neither.

key_moments.quote: We are both home now being retired, so we are using more energy than we did when we worked.
key_moments.stewart_read: Doug is describing a structural load increase — two retired adults home all day, plus a 1,500 sq ft shop and garage. This is a high-consumption profile that makes the solar case stronger, not weaker. Carter responds with 'Okay. Gotcha.' and moves to qualifiers. The flip opportunity here is distinct from the fixed-income disclosure: Doug has just explained WHY his bill is high and WHY it will stay high. That is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch.

key_moments.quote: I have memory problems, so I don't pay a lot of bills.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the first explicit cognitive-decline disclosure. Carter's response ('You're good. Yeah. No worries.') is a social reassurance, not an acknowledgment. More critically, Carter does not register the structural implication: if Doug has memory problems and doesn't pay the bills, the callback plan that will be constructed in the next two minutes — which depends on Doug relaying a message to his wife — is already compromised. This is both an empathy miss and a planning failure.

key_moments.quote: You could, email her at simplygottarelax@gmail.com.
key_moments.stewart_read: The wife's email is the only confirmed direct contact path to the decision-maker on this call. Carter accepts it as a bill-collection address ('I'll shoot my...') but never uses it as an appointment-setting anchor. The email could have been the vehicle for a locked callback confirmation — 'I'll send her an email right now with a couple of times that work, and she can pick one' — which would have bypassed the Doug-relay dependency entirely. Instead it becomes a passive follow-up address.
